Speaking of tenant headwinds, which I discussed more in-depth last month, due to the REIT preferring to focus on non-investment rated tenants, a recession does serve as a serious risk.

Their tenants are smaller, lesser-known, and not as financially stable as retailers like Walmart (WMT) & Costco (COST), who Agree Realty (ADC) typically leases to.

But despite tenant headwinds which impacted NNN's occupancy, dropping year-over-year from 99.5% to 98.5%, NNN still managed to see solid AFFO growth of 2.8%. And for 2025 they are still expected to see some growth, albeit lower at 1.9%.

AFFO is expected in a range of $3.39 - $3.44, up from $3.35 in 2024. Investments are expected to be solid in a range of $500 million - $600 million for the year.

However, the macro environment could impact this going forward, ultimately suppressing growth, negatively affecting their share price.

But at a forward P/AFFO multiple of just 11.62x, NNN trades well-below their normal valuation of 15.59x. They also trade below their peer average of 13.86x, signaling they could be undervalued.

Moreover, if the FED does lower interest rates due to a recession, NNN's share price could be affected positively as REIT share prices typically move in the opposite direction of interest rates.

And despite NNN REIT not being considered a high-growth stock, their conservative payout ratio, robust liquidity, and low valuation make them a great pick for a retiree.

Why Ares Capital?

Ares Capital's, a somewhat riskier income play being a BDC, recent sell-off makes them attractive once again.

Due to BDCs lending to smaller, and sometimes financially distressed businesses, they are viewed as attractive for income investors & retirees, but riskier for growth, risk-adverse investors.

But why I'm recommending Ares Capital today, despite a recession potentially impacting their financials by causing rising non-performing loans and PIK income, is their conservative management team.

While many BDCs were rewarding shareholders with special dividends and increases, ARCC elected to hold their dividend steady at $0.48 since late 2022.

One reason could be their externally managed structure, but in my opinion, has more to do with their forward-looking management team. The last two years, ARCC prioritized growth instead of dividend raises, which positioned them favorably compared to peers.

Their top and bottom lines grew solidly year-over-year from $2.6 billion and $1.266 billion to $2.99 billion and $1.4 billion. Their double-digit bottom line growth resulted in net asset value growth from $19.24 to $19.89 per share.

So, while their portfolio may not be as defensively-positioned at 57% first-lien loans compared to their externally-managed peers like Blackstone Secured Lending's (BXSL) 98.7% and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending's (MSDL) 96%, ARCC's superior growth offsets this.

Additionally, their liquidity was robust, with $635 million in cash available and $5 billion capacity on their revolver. This is more than enough to satisfy their upcoming debt this year of $1.85 billion. Their leverage of just 0.99x was also well-below the peer average.

They also had a strong pipeline coming into 2025 with $1.2 billion in commitments, up 80% year-over-year, with a $1.8 billion backlog.

ARCC's non-performing borrowers have held up well in the past year despite higher for longer interest rates. These stood at 1.7% at cost and 0.9% at fair value, up from 1.3% and 0.6%, respectively.

And while these would likely rise in a recession, I expect ARCC to maintain their current dividend of $0.48, which they've paid for 15 years.

Ares Capital is also known to rollover ample spillover income into the upcoming year. At the end of this year, this stood at $922 million, or $1.37, roughly 3 quarters worth of dividends.

This could be used to make investments for growth, or for dividends should coverage become impacted from potential borrower loan defaults. And with ARCC's conservative management team and long track record, I suspect their dividend will remain safe.

Moreover, at a price of $19.99 at the time of writing, which they haven't traded near since late 2023, this gives the BDC a very attractive P/NAV ratio of roughly 1x. Wall Street also rates the stock a buy with a $23.35 price target.

While BDCs are seen as higher risk investments as a result of recession threats, I suspect volatility could increase in the coming months. Moreover, upside could be limited as the uncertainty surrounding interest rates due to recession fears.

If so, this will likely have a negative impact on BDCs. If rates remain higher with no additional cuts this year, I see upside for ARCC.

But as previously mentioned, ARCC's superior growth, ample liquidity, and share price below NAV makes them an attractive income play in either scenario. Additionally, their conservative management ensures their 9.7% yield will likely remain safe for the near to medium-term.

Bottom Line

Despite recession risks, which could cause pain & discomfort, both NNN REIT & Ares Capital are conservative with robust liquidity available that will likely ensure their yields remain safe, at least for the near to medium term.

Additionally, both have respectable dividend track records, 35 years and 15 years respectively, that I anticipate their management teams will work to ensure remain intact despite economic volatility.

Despite limited upside due to increased recession fears, both have strong investment-grade balance sheets and robust liquidity available to absorb the impact from distressed tenants should a recession come to fruition, or we see stagflation.

That's why it's imperative to focus on stocks with strong fundamentals who trade at attractive valuations to limit downside and continue to pay dividends to grow your income stream. Both NNN REIT and Ares Capital currently fit the bill.

data.title: Brinker International: Unjustified Sell-Off, Strong Comps May Continue
data.description:
Brinker International (NYSE:EAT) experienced strong growth over the past year as the company achieved record same-store sales growth of 31% for Chili's in Q2 FY25. EAT also achieved 600 basis points of operating margin expansion for the quarter.

While Brinker's achievements may not be as strong in future quarters in 2025, I do think that the company has a chance for further strong growth. The reason for that is because its Chili's restaurants comprise 89% of total company sales. Chili's restaurants are affordable options for dining out. So, I think the company can continue to achieve same-store sales gains as the year progresses.

The gains in future quarters may not be as high as in Q2 due to economic/market uncertainty, but Chili's does provide a good option for families to dine out without breaking the bank. Therefore, I think Brinker has a good chance to perform well in 2025.

I realize that Brinker's Maggiano's restaurants are a little pricier than Chili's, but Maggiano's only comprises 11% of total revenue. So, Maggiano's may not drag Brinker down that much if consumers trade down to more affordable options.

Brinker's Growth Catalysts

Brinker's recent success is largely due to the Chili's restaurants, which were ranked as the number one casual dining chain in the industry in 2024. This success was attributed to Generation Z, where sales among this group increased 23.5%. Brinker's emphasis on value probably led to these sales increases. Another factor could be the popularity of the Chili's Triple Dipper appetizer menu, which has been posted heavily on social media.

It is important to point out that Brinker bucked the trend of its industry over the past year. Chili's sales increased 8.9% in 2024 as compared to the industry's 3.1% decline. This demonstrates that Chili's resonates with consumers, probably due to its reasonable prices.

Brinker has plans to keep the positive momentum going, which may lead to strong performance for 2025. The company made recipe changes for a higher quality experience. This includes guacamole, which is freshly made each day, higher-quality chicken breasts, and by making its bone-in wings and bacon crispier. So far, EAT's improvements led to higher guest satisfaction scores.

The company also removed 13 menu items to focus on improving the existing options. Brinker also installed new kitchen display systems, which led to faster service for customers. Brinker is in the process of upgrading the remainder of its ovens to TurboChefs which accelerate cooking times. The TurboChef ovens yield crispier items such as ribs and quesadillas, save space, are more reliable and easier to clean.

Brinker made upgrades to the Maggiano's menu. This includes a freshly made Caesar salad, an upgraded meat sauce, a new Fettuccine Alfredo, a crispier chicken parmesan, a new meatball recipe, and a 30-layer meat & sausage lasagna.

Brinker also mentioned that Maggiano's has plans to improve its service efficiency and is evaluating its real estate. No specifics were given yet on these two initiatives.

Increasing margins are another growth driver for Brinker. The company increased its gross margin to 19.8% in Q2 FY25, as compared to 14% from Q2 FY24. The net income margin increased from 3.9% to 8.7% over the same time period. The margin gains were largely attributed to sales leverage from top line growth. Margin increases have a good chance to continue from Brinker's focus on more efficient operations, plus from more expected sales gains.

Brinker is expected to achieve total revenue growth of 18% for FY25, which ends in June, according to analysts' consensus estimates. This would result in total revenue of $5.23 billion for the fiscal year. EAT appears to be on the right track to achieve this with its positive sales momentum.

Valuation

Brinker is trading at 17x expected EPS of $8.35 for FY25 and at 15x expected EPS of $9.50 for FY26. This is much lower than the Restaurant industry's forward PE of 23x. Wingstop (WING) has a similar market cap and is overvalued with a forward PE of 63x.

Brinker had 20 upward earnings revisions over the past 3 months. Wingstop had 24 downward earnings revisions over the same period. Wingstop's earnings are expected to decline by -0.05% for FY25. Brinker is expected to grow earnings at an impressive 103% to 104% for FY25 and follow that up with a 14% increase for FY26.

Brinker's expected EPS of $8.35 for FY25 can be achieved with continued strong same-store sales to drive the expected total revenue growth of 18%. The strong margin increases that EAT has been achieving has the potential to drive earnings to hit the consensus EPS estimate.

Brinker looks like one of the better restaurant stocks in the industry in terms of a reasonable valuation and future expected growth. The company has been executing effectively, which has been reflected in its same-store sales and margin increases.

Technical Perspective

EAT's daily chart above shows the decline that the stock experienced in 2025. The good news is that the decline is not a result of any company-specific issue. The stock was just a victim of the market's broad sell-off. The other good news is that there is a bullish divergence between the price of the stock which declined from March into April while the RSI and MACD increased over the same period.

This looks like a bottoming of the stock. These set-ups typically result in a reversal for the stock back to positive from what I have observed. A new uptrend will be confirmed when the RSI moves above 50 and the blue MACD line crosses above the red signal line.

Brinker's Q3 FY25 earnings report scheduled for April 29, 2025 is the next likely catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting Brinker to achieve $1.38 billion in revenue and normalized EPS of $2.48 for the quarter. It should be interesting to see how same-store sales turns out. Another strong same-store sales increase would make it likely for Brinker to meet/exceed these estimates. The comps may not be as strong as they were in Q2, but they don't have to be that high to meet the estimates. They may need about 20% same-store sales gains to hit the estimates for Q3.

What is interesting is that Brinker stated in the Q2 earnings call question & answer session that the company is sustaining the high comps. So, it is possible that the company exceeds analysts' estimates in Q3.

Risks for Brinker

The expectations for Brinker are high for Q3 based on the success in recent quarters. It is possible that the company won't meet analysts' expectations for the next few quarters. Same-store sales growth could be significantly lower than recent quarters. Consumers could change their preferences to favor other restaurants or by preparing more food at home. This could have a negative impact on revenue and earnings and drive the stock lower.

High profile food poisoning incidents at Brinker's restaurants could lead to a decline in sales. There are various food-borne illnesses such as salmonella, norovirus, E. coli, etc. that could infect the company's customers. Negative publicity from these possible infections could cause consumers to avoid Brinker's restaurants.

Brinker has a high concentration of restaurants in certain states. Brinker has 18.8% of its restaurants in Texas, 11.8% in Florida, and 9.2% in California. Negative publicity, illness outbreaks, large natural disasters, or significant changes in consumer preferences in those states could have a large negative impact on sales.

Brinker's Long-Term Outlook

Brinker's turnaround has been highly successful. The company appears confident that the high same-store sales that were achieved in recent quarters can be sustained. If this is achieved over the remainder of FY25 and into FY26, the stock has a good chance to outperform many other restaurant companies in the industry.

The unjustified sell-off for the stock because of the broader market decline has the stock at a reasonable valuation. This leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock. Analysts have a one-year price target of $180 for Brinker. This would result in a 25% gain. That would take the PE to 19 based on expected EPS of $9.50 for FY26. That's still below the industry's average forward PE of 23.
